critique.summary: The article provides a detailed glossary of Japanese chopsticks etiquette, outlining various actions considered rude or taboo while eating in Japan. It explains specific chopsticks faux pas, known as kiraibashi, with cultural and sometimes religious reasons behind these customs.
mainPoints: Certain ways of using chopsticks are considered bad manners or taboo in Japan, collectively called kiraibashi.
Examples include passing food from chopsticks to chopsticks, standing chopsticks upright in rice, and cleaning chopsticks in soup, each with cultural or religious significance.
The article lists numerous specific faux pas, explaining why each is considered inappropriate in Japanese dining etiquette.
Some actions are linked to Buddhist funeral customs, making them especially serious taboos.
Proper chopsticks etiquette involves respectful handling and avoiding behaviors that may be seen as disrespectful or unhygienic.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reflects well-documented aspects of Japanese dining etiquette related to chopsticks use. The described taboos such as passing food chopsticks-to-chopsticks and standing chopsticks upright in rice are widely recognized in Japanese culture and have clear cultural and religious origins. The glossary format and detailed explanations align with established etiquette guides and cultural studies on Japanese dining manners. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, which would strengthen its reliability as journalism.
considerations: The article does not cite any sources or experts, which would help verify the accuracy and provide authority.
It does not discuss regional variations or how strictly these rules are followed in contemporary Japan.
No perspectives on how foreigners are expected to adapt or how these customs have evolved over time are provided.
The article does not mention the context of informal versus formal dining, where some rules might be relaxed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the cultural nuances and etiquette of using chopsticks in Japan, with commenters sharing personal experiences, clarifications, and some skepticism about the strictness or relevance of certain rules. There is a mix of appreciation for the cultural insights and questioning of how strictly these faux pas are observed in everyday life.

comments.author: cthalupa
comments.text:
Interesting. Some of these are big deals (particularly the ones mentioned as important) but others I have seen Japanese people in Tokyo do quite consistently. Soroebashi - not on the table, but I've seen chopsticks aligned by pushing them against the plate hundreds of time. I've also seen them used to stir miso soup, etc. plenty.

Others I don't know that I would have much of an inclination to do and haven't seen but am not sure if it's because it really is a faux pas or just because no one else really tends to do it either.
comments.analysis: Provides firsthand observations that suggest some rules are flexible in practice, adding nuance to the discussion.

comments.author: unsignedint
comments.text:
The article does a good job calling out the more serious offenses, although I’d personally argue that nigiribashi is just as bad as the other two. Most Japanese people would probably react with a bit of shock to those.

That said, chopstick etiquette is definitely evolving. Something like chobujubashi isn’t enforced as strictly anymore, especially with more awareness around left-handed users. Kaeshibashi, on the other hand, is becoming more common, and in some social circles, not doing it can actually come across as rude.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced view acknowledging cultural evolution and varying social acceptance of certain etiquette rules.

comments.author: emursebrian
comments.text:
Most of these are common sense. As a tourist foreigner, you also aren't expected to know all the customs but it's appreciated when you try. The one about which direction to NOT point the chopsticks in was new to me. If you just watch what other people are doing, then try to do the same thing, you're probably on the right track.

Related to eating, one pro-tip I got from a local is that when you're ready to close your tab or get your check at a bar or restaurant, you can make a small X with your index fingers.

Really useful in a busy bar!
comments.analysis: Practical advice emphasizing cultural sensitivity and observation, which aligns well with social norms.

comments.author: vunderba
comments.text:
When I first moved to Taiwan and was just getting a handle on Chinese, I asked a waiter "請給我一個筷子" - not yet being familiar with proper measure words.

The waiter (who had a bit of a sense of humor) brought me exactly ONE chopstick. I laughed and repeated 請給我另一個筷子 (Please give me another chopstick) and he brought out another one.

Of course later my friend told me that I should have used 雙 to indicate I wanted a "pair" of chopsticks.
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant anecdote that highlights cultural and linguistic nuances around chopstick usage.

comments.author: AftHurrahWinch
comments.text: Phew, I'm glad "inserting them into your nostrils and braying like a walrus" isn't on the list.
comments.analysis: Lighthearted comment that adds humor but little substantive value to the discussion.

comments.author: mijoharas
comments.text:
For anyone else curious after reading "-bashi" 40 times:

(Not gonna direct quote because the damn site doesn't allow copy-pasting so they don't get a link, paraphrased):

Kirai-bashi would be literally translated to "dislike-chopsticks" and means bad chopstick table-manners. Hashi is chopsticks and bashi is the voiced form of it.

So the bashi suffix/word on the end of all of these just means chopsticks it seems.
comments.analysis: Helpful linguistic clarification that aids understanding of the article's terminology.

comments.author: mjamesaustin
comments.text: I was shocked to find it's a faux pas to rub disposable chopsticks to remove potential splinters. I was taught this is what you're supposed to do with disposable chopsticks.
comments.analysis: Raises a common misconception, reflecting cultural differences in chopstick handling practices.

comments.author: perdomon
comments.text: Some of these sound just as made-up as a lot of Western dining "rules." Maybe someone more familiar with the culture can say whether or not these are true faux pas in an everyday ramen shop or similar.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the rigidity of some etiquette rules, encouraging cultural context consideration.

comments.author: tempodox
comments.text:
Highly instructive, and some quite surprising to me as a gaijin.

> To take the tips of the chopsticks in one’s mouth.

Sometimes I'm having a hard time avoiding that. Apparently I need more practice.
comments.analysis: Shows genuine engagement with the etiquette and personal reflection on learning the customs.

comments.author: bigwheels
comments.text:
Fascinating culture and raises numerous questions arising from my subsequent confusion:

1. > 返し箸 Kaeshibashi (also known as 逆さ箸 sakasabashi)

> To turn the chopsticks around when serving food so that the tips of the chopsticks that have touched one’s mouth do not touch the food.

Does this mean it is preferable to use the tips that may have touched mouth to then serve more food? Or is this considered fine because it's also taboo to touch the tips to your mouth? (which only a BARBARIAN would do!)

2. > こすり箸 Kosuribashi

> To rub waribashi (disposable chopsticks) together to remove splinters.

Just proceed to eat some splinters, then? What is the good etiquette way to handle low quality el-cheapo chopsticks?

---

I have been guilty of the above as well as:

Chigiribashi - Hold one chopstick in each hand and use them like a knife and fork to tear or cut food into smaller pieces.

Soroebashi - Hold chopsticks together and tap them on a dish or the top of the table to align the tips.

Namidabashi - Allow sauce or soup to drip from the tips of the chopsticks when eating. Namida means “tears.”

Nigiribashi - Grip both chopsticks in a fist.

Neburibashi - Lick the chopsticks.

Hashibashi - Place the chopsticks like a bridge across the top of a dish to show one is finished. Chopsticks should be placed on the hashioki (chopstick rest).

Furibashi - Shake off soup, sauce, or small bits of food from the tips of the chopsticks.

Mogibashi - Bite off and eat grains of rice that are stuck to the chopsticks.

Yokobashi - Line the chopsticks up together and use them like a spoon to scoop up food.

.. growing up my mom used to say, "What are you, raised by wolves!?" .. apparently, yes!
comments.analysis: Provides detailed examples of common confusions, contributing to deeper understanding of etiquette complexities.
